title: Mean and standard deviation of difference of sample means
description: Practice calculating the mean and standard deviation of sampling distributions for differences in sample means.
topic_path: Sampling distributions for differences in sample means
Mean and standard deviation of difference of sample means

assessment_items.question_content:
Suppose that Amanda raises chickens whose eggs have a mean weight of $57$ grams and a standard deviation of $6$ grams. Her friend Bernardo also raises chickens, and their eggs have a mean weight of $54$ grams and a standard deviation of $8$ grams.

They decide to have a contest where they'll each randomly select $60$ of their eggs, calculate the mean weight, and look at the difference between their sample means $(\bar{x}_\text{A}-\bar{x}_\text{B})$.

**What are the mean and standard deviation (in grams) of the sampling distribution of $\bar{x}_\text{A}-\bar{x}_\text{B}$?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
###Formulas

The mean of the sampling distribution of $\bar{x}_1-\bar{x}_2$ is the difference of the population means:

$\mu_{\bar{x}_1-\bar{x}_2}=\mu_1-\mu_2$

The standard deviation of $\bar{x}_1-\bar{x}_2$ is: 

$\sigma_{\bar{x}_1-\bar{x}_2}=\sqrt{\dfrac{\sigma_1^2}{n_1}+\dfrac{\sigma_2^2}{n_2}}$

(where $n_1$ and $n_2$ are the sizes of each sample).

###Identifying values

"...Amanda raises chickens whose eggs have a mean weight of $\blueE{57}$ grams and a standard deviation of $\redE{6}$ grams...Bernardo also raises chickens, and their eggs have a mean weight of $\maroonE{54}$ grams and a standard deviation of $\goldE{8}$ grams...they'll each randomly select $\purpleD{60}$ of their eggs..."

Person | Mean (g) | Std. dev. (g) | Sample size
:- | :-: | :-: | :-:
Amanda | $\blueE{\mu_\text{A}}=\blueE{57}$ | $\redE{\sigma_\text{A}}=\redE{6}$ | $\purpleD{n_\text{A}}=\purpleD{60}$
Bernardo | $\maroonD{\mu_\text{B}}=\maroonD{54}$ | $\goldE{\sigma_\text{B}}=\goldE{8}$ | $\purpleD{n_\text{B}}=\purpleD{60}$

assessment_items.question_content:
Amber and Shinji both play a single-player game where they try to survive as many rounds as possible without crashing a bird into an obstacle. Here's the historical data for how many rounds each player survives in a single attempt at this game:

Player | Shape | Mean | Std. dev.
- | - | -: | -:
Amber | Normal | $35.5$ | $7.1$
Shinji | Normal | $30.0$ | $8.6$

They decide to each play $10$ attempts and calculate the sample mean of how many rounds they survive. They will then look at the difference in their sample means $\left( \bar{x}_\text{A} - \bar{x}_\text{S} \right)$.

**What are the mean and standard deviation (in rounds) of the sampling distribution of $\bar{x}_\text{A}-\bar{x}_\text{S}$?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
###Formulas

The mean of the sampling distribution of $\bar{x}_1-\bar{x}_2$ is the difference of the population means:

$\mu_{\bar{x}_1-\bar{x}_2}=\mu_1-\mu_2$

The standard deviation of $\bar{x}_1-\bar{x}_2$ is: 

$\sigma_{\bar{x}_1-\bar{x}_2}=\sqrt{\dfrac{\sigma_1^2}{n_1}+\dfrac{\sigma_2^2}{n_2}}$

(where $n_1$ and $n_2$ are the sizes of each sample).

###Identifying values

"...They decide to each play $\purpleD{10}$ attempts."

The rest of the values we need are in the table:

Player | Mean | Std. dev. | Sample size
- | -: | -: | -:
Amber | $\blueE{\mu_\text{A}}=\blueE{35.5}$ | $\redE{\sigma_\text{A}}=\redE{7.1}$ | $\purpleD{n_\text{A}}=\purpleD{10}$
Shinji | $\maroonD{\mu_\text{S}}=\maroonD{30.0}$ | $\goldE{\sigma_\text{S}}=\goldE{8.6}$ | $\purpleD{n_\text{S}}=\purpleD{10}$

assessment_items.question_content:
A company has offices in two different countries. Suppose that the average age (in years) of the employees at location A is $40.0$ with a standard deviation of $5.5$, and the average at location B is $36.0$ with a standard deviation of $6.3$.

Every month, the company takes separate random samples of $50$ employees from location A and $75$ from location B for a survey. Each time, they look at the difference in the mean age sampled from each location $\left( \bar{x}_\text{A} - \bar{x}_\text{B} \right)$.

**What are the mean and standard deviation (in years) of the sampling distribution of $\bar{x}_\text{A}-\bar{x}_\text{B}$?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
###Formulas

The mean of the sampling distribution of $\bar{x}_1-\bar{x}_2$ is the difference of the population means:

$\mu_{\bar{x}_1-\bar{x}_2}=\mu_1-\mu_2$

The standard deviation of $\bar{x}_1-\bar{x}_2$ is: 

$\sigma_{\bar{x}_1-\bar{x}_2}=\sqrt{\dfrac{\sigma_1^2}{n_1}+\dfrac{\sigma_2^2}{n_2}}$

(where $n_1$ and $n_2$ are the sizes of each sample).

###Identifying values

"...the average age (in years) of the employees at location A is $\blueE{40.0}$ with a standard deviation of $\goldE{5.5}$, and the average at location B is $\maroonD{36.0}$ with a standard deviation of $\tealE{6.3}$...the company takes separate random samples of $\redE{50}$ employees from location A and $\purpleD{75}$ from location B..."

Location | Mean | Std. dev. | Sample size
:- | :-: | :-: | :-:
A | $\blueE{\mu_\text{A}}=\blueE{40.0}$ | $\goldE{\sigma_\text{A}}=\goldE{5.5}$ | $\redE{n_\text{A}}=\redE{50}$
B | $\maroonD{\mu_\text{B}}=\maroonD{36.0}$ | $\tealE{\sigma_\text{B}}=\tealE{6.3}$ | $\purpleD{n_\text{B}}=\purpleD{75}$

assessment_items.question_content:
At Lakeview High School, students either walk to school or provide their own transportation. Suppose that those who walk have a mean commute time of $12$ minutes with a standard deviation of $5$ minutes, while those who use some other form of travel have a mean and standard deviation of $16$ and $6$ minutes, respectively.

The principal takes an SRS of $50$ students who walk, and an independent SRS of $30$ students who use some other form of travel. The principle calculates the mean commute time for each sample, then looks at the difference $(\text{Other}-\text{Walk})$ between the sample means.

**What are the mean and standard deviation (in minutes) of the sampling distribution of the difference in sample means?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
###Formulas

The mean of the sampling distribution of $\bar{x}_1-\bar{x}_2$ is the difference of the population means:

$\mu_{\bar{x}_1-\bar{x}_2}=\mu_1-\mu_2$

The standard deviation of $\bar{x}_1-\bar{x}_2$ is: 

$\sigma_{\bar{x}_1-\bar{x}_2}=\sqrt{\dfrac{\sigma_1^2}{n_1}+\dfrac{\sigma_2^2}{n_2}}$

(where $n_1$ and $n_2$ are the sizes of each sample).

###Identifying values

"...those who walk have a mean commute time of $\maroonD{12}$ minutes with a standard deviation of $\tealE{5}$ minutes...those who use some other form of travel have a mean and standard deviation of $\blueE{16}$ and $\goldE{6}$ minutes, respectively...the principal takes an SRS of $\purpleD{50}$ students who walk, and an independent SRS of $\redE{30}$ students who use some other form of travel."

Travel type | Mean (minutes) | Std. dev. (minutes) | Sample size
:- | :-: | :-: | :-:
Other | $\blueE{\mu_\text{O}}=\blueE{16}$ | $\goldE{\sigma_\text{O}}=\goldE{6}$ | $\redE{n_\text{O}}=\redE{30}$
Walk | $\maroonD{\mu_\text{W}}=\maroonD{12}$ | $\tealE{\sigma_\text{W}}=\tealE{5}$ | $\purpleD{n_\text{W}}=\purpleD{50}$

assessment_items.question_content:
People at an airport can pass through security on one of two levels: level A or level B. Suppose that, on average, it takes people $22$ minutes to pass through security on level A with a standard deviation of $7$ minutes. On level B, the mean and standard deviation are $20$ and $6$ minutes, respectively.

Every day, the airport looks at an SRS of $100$ people who use level A, and an independent SRS of $150$ people who use level B. They calculate the mean time for each sample, then look at the difference $(\text{A}-\text{B})$ between the sample means.

**What are the mean and standard deviation (in minutes) of the sampling distribution of the difference in sample means?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
###Formulas

The mean of the sampling distribution of $\bar{x}_1-\bar{x}_2$ is the difference of the population means:

$\mu_{\bar{x}_1-\bar{x}_2}=\mu_1-\mu_2$

The standard deviation of $\bar{x}_1-\bar{x}_2$ is: 

$\sigma_{\bar{x}_1-\bar{x}_2}=\sqrt{\dfrac{\sigma_1^2}{n_1}+\dfrac{\sigma_2^2}{n_2}}$

(where $n_1$ and $n_2$ are the sizes of each sample).

###Identifying values

"...on average, it takes people $\blueE{22}$ minutes to pass through security on level A with a standard deviation of $\goldE{7}$ minutes. On level B, the mean and standard deviation are $\maroonD{20}$ and $\tealE{6}$ minutes, respectively...the airport looks at an SRS of $\redE{100}$ people who use level A, and an independent SRS of $\purpleD{150}$ people who use level B."

Level | Mean (minutes) | Std. dev. (minutes) | Sample size
:- | :-: | :-: | :-:
A | $\blueE{\mu_\text{A}}=\blueE{22}$ | $\goldE{\sigma_\text{A}}=\goldE{7}$ | $\redE{n_\text{A}}=\redE{100}$
B | $\maroonD{\mu_\text{B}}=\maroonD{20}$ | $\tealE{\sigma_\text{B}}=\tealE{6}$ | $\purpleD{n_\text{B}}=\purpleD{150}$

assessment_items.question_content:
A large bakery makes cakes in two shifts: shift A and shift B. Suppose that, on average, cakes from shift A weigh $7.4\,\text{kg}$ with a standard deviation of $0.2\,\text{kg}$. For shift B, the mean and standard deviation are $7.0\,\text{kg}$ and $0.1\,\text{kg}$, respectively.

Every day, the bakery takes an SRS of $25$ cakes from each shift. They calculate the mean weight for each sample, then look at the difference $(\text{A}-\text{B})$ between the sample means.

**What are the mean and standard deviation (in kilograms) of the sampling distribution of $\bar{x}_\text{A}-\bar{x}_\text{B}$?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
###Formulas

The mean of the sampling distribution of $\bar{x}_1-\bar{x}_2$ is the difference of the population means:

$\mu_{\bar{x}_1-\bar{x}_2}=\mu_1-\mu_2$

The standard deviation of $\bar{x}_1-\bar{x}_2$ is: 

$\sigma_{\bar{x}_1-\bar{x}_2}=\sqrt{\dfrac{\sigma_1^2}{n_1}+\dfrac{\sigma_2^2}{n_2}}$

(where $n_1$ and $n_2$ are the sizes of each sample).

###Identifying values

"...on average, cakes from shift A weigh $\blueE{7.4\,\text{kg}}$ with a standard deviation of $\redE{0.2\,\text{kg}}$. For shift B, the mean and standard deviation are $\maroonD{7.0\,\text{kg}}$ and $\goldE{0.1\,\text{kg}}$, respectively...the bakery takes an SRS of $\purpleD{25}$ cakes from each shift."

Shift | Mean (kg) | Std. dev. (kg) | Sample size
:- | :-: | :-: | :-:
A | $\blueE{\mu_\text{A}}=\blueE{7.4}$ | $\redE{\sigma_\text{A}}=\redE{0.2}$ | $\purpleD{n_\text{A}}=\purpleD{25}$
B | $\maroonD{\mu_\text{B}}=\maroonD{7.0}$ | $\goldE{\sigma_\text{B}}=\goldE{0.1}$ | $\purpleD{n_\text{B}}=\purpleD{25}$

assessment_items.question_content:
A car manufacturer has two production plants in different cities. Every day, plant A produces $120$ of a certain type of car, while plant B produces $80$ of those cars. On average, all of these cars have a paint thickness of $0.04\,\text{mm}$ with a standard deviation of $0.003\,\text{mm}$.

Every day, quality control experts take separate random samples of $10$ cars from each plant and calculate the mean paint thickness for each sample. They then look at the difference between those sample means.

Consider the formula:  

$\sigma_{\bar{x}_1-\bar{x}_2}=\sqrt{\dfrac{\sigma_1^2}{n_1}+\dfrac{\sigma_\text{2}^2}{n_2}}$

**Why is it not appropriate to use this formula for the standard deviation of $\bar{x}_\text{A}-\bar{x}_\text{B}$?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
###Independence conditions

To use this formula for $\sigma_{\bar{x}_\text{A}-\bar{x}_\text{B}}$, we need two types of independence:

1. **Independent samples**: This formula relies on adding the variances of $\bar{x}_\text{A}$ and $\bar{x}_\text{B}$, and we learned that we can only add variances when we have independent random variables. In other words, knowledge of one sample shouldn't give us extra information about the other sample.
2. **Independent observations within each sample**: Each observation in a sample shouldn't give us extra information about the next. If we're sampling without replacement, and we're sampling less than $10\%$ of the population, we can treat members in our sample as independent from each other. We call this the $10\%$ condition.

In this setting, are the samples independent? Are the members in each sample independent?

###Independent samples

The plants are in different cities, and they are taking separate random samples, so results from one sample wouldn't give them extra information about the other sample.

It's reasonable to treat these as independent samples.

###Independence within plant A sample

They sample $10$ cars per day from plant A. To pass the $10\%$ condition there would need to be at least $100$ cars in that population, and they make $120$ cars each day at this plant.

So it's reasonable to treat cars in this sample as being independent.

###Independence within plant B sample

They sample $10$ cars per day from plant B. To pass the $10\%$ condition there would need to be at least $100$ cars in that population, but they only make $80$ cars each day at this plant.

So we **cannot** treat cars in this sample as being independent. They would need to take a smaller sample, or better yet, use a different formula that is beyond the scope of this course.

[[☃ explanation 1]]

###Answer

It is not appropriate to use this standard deviation formula because we cannot assume independence for the cars sampled from plant B.

choices.content: The samples are not independent of each other.
We cannot assume independence for the cars sampled from plant A.
We cannot assume independence for the cars sampled from plant B.

assessment_items.question_content:
A company has offices in two different countries. The $125$ employees at location A have a mean commute time of $40$ minutes and a standard deviation of $15$ minutes. The $250$ employees at location B have a mean and standard deviation of $35$ and $10$ minutes, respectively.

Every month, the company takes separate random samples of $20$ employees from each location for a survey. Each time, they look at the difference in the mean commute time sampled from each location $\left( \bar{x}_\text{A} - \bar{x}_\text{B} \right)$.

Consider the formula:  

$\sigma_{\bar{x}_1-\bar{x}_2}=\sqrt{\dfrac{\sigma_1^2}{n_1}+\dfrac{\sigma_\text{2}^2}{n_2}}$

**Why is it not appropriate to use this formula for the standard deviation of $\bar{x}_\text{A}-\bar{x}_\text{B}$?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
###Independence conditions

To use this formula for $\sigma_{\bar{x}_\text{A}-\bar{x}_\text{B}}$, we need two types of independence:

1. **Independent samples**: This formula relies on adding the variances of $\bar{x}_\text{A}$ and $\bar{x}_\text{B}$, and we learned that we can only add variances when we have independent random variables. In other words, knowledge of one sample shouldn't give us extra information about the other sample.
2. **Independent observations within each sample**: Each observation in a sample shouldn't give us extra information about the next. If we're sampling without replacement, and we're sampling less than $10\%$ of the population, we can treat members in our sample as independent from each other. We call this the $10\%$ condition.

In this setting, are the samples independent? Are the members in each sample independent?

###Independent samples

The locations are in different countries, and they are taking separate random samples, so results from one sample wouldn't give them extra information about the other sample.

It's reasonable to treat these as independent samples.

###Independence within location A sample

They sample $20$ employees each month from location A. To pass the $10\%$ condition there would need to be at least $200$ employees in that population, but there are only $125$ employees at location A.

So we **cannot** treat employees in this sample as being independent. They would need to take a smaller sample, or better yet, use a different formula that is beyond the scope of this course.

[[☃ explanation 1]]

###Answer

It is not appropriate to use this standard deviation formula because we cannot assume independence for the employees sampled from location A.

choices.content: The samples are not independent of each other.
We cannot assume independence for the employees sampled from location A.
We cannot assume independence for the employees sampled from location B.

assessment_items.question_content:
A reading society allows its members to request books, then pick up their books at the library or have them delivered to their home. Here is the historical data for number of books per year for members who choose each option.

Pickup location | Mean | Std. dev. | Total members 
- | - | -: | -: | -:
Library | $15.7$ | $11.3$ | $252$
Home | $13.5$ | $8.1$ | $147$

The society leaders take separate random samples of $24$ members from each option. They will then look at the difference in their sample means $\left( \bar{x}_\text{L} - \bar{x}_\text{H} \right)$.

Consider the formula:  

$\sigma_{\bar{x}_1-\bar{x}_2}=\sqrt{\dfrac{\sigma_1^2}{n_1}+\dfrac{\sigma_\text{2}^2}{n_2}}$

**Why is it not appropriate to use this formula for the standard deviation of $\bar{x}_\text{L}-\bar{x}_\text{H}$?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
###Independence conditions

To use this formula for $\sigma_{\bar{x}_\text{L}-\bar{x}_\text{H}}$, we need two types of independence:

1. **Independent samples**: This formula relies on adding the variances of $\bar{x}_\text{L}$ and $\bar{x}_\text{H}$, and we learned that we can only add variances when we have independent random variables. In other words, knowledge of one sample shouldn't give us extra information about the other sample.
2. **Independent observations within each sample**: Each observation in a sample shouldn't give us extra information about the next. If we're sampling without replacement, and we're sampling less than $10\%$ of the population, we can treat members in our sample as independent from each other. We call this the $10\%$ condition.

In this setting, are the samples independent? Are the members in each sample independent?

###Independent samples

They are taking separate random samples of different types of members, so results from one sample wouldn't give them extra information about the other sample.

It's reasonable to treat these as independent samples.

###Independence within library option sample

They sample $24$ members who chose the library option. To pass the $10\%$ condition there would need to be at least $240$ members in that population, and there are $252$ members who chose this option.

So it's reasonable to treat members in this sample as being independent.

###Independence within home option sample

They sample $24$ members who chose the home option. To pass the $10\%$ condition there would need to be at least $240$ members in that population, but there are only $147$ members who chose this option.

So we **cannot** treat members in this sample as being independent. They would need to take a smaller sample, or better yet, use a different formula that is beyond the scope of this course.

[[☃ explanation 1]]

###Answer

It is not appropriate to use this standard deviation formula because we cannot assume independence for the members  sampled from the home option.

choices.content: The samples are not independent of each other.
We cannot assume independence for the members  sampled from the library option.
We cannot assume independence for the members  sampled from the home option.

assessment_items.question_content:
Administrators at International University are curious how students' GPAs after their first year compare to their high school GPAs. They plan on taking an SRS of $80$ of the $900$ freshmen to look up their high school GPAs. They plan to look at the difference between the mean high school and the mean university GPA for students in the sample $(\bar{x}_\text{HS}-\bar{x}_\text{Uni})$ at the end of their first year.

Consider the formula:  

$\sigma_{\bar{x}_1-\bar{x}_2}=\sqrt{\dfrac{\sigma_1^2}{n_1}+\dfrac{\sigma_\text{2}^2}{n_2}}$

**Why is it not appropriate to use this formula for the standard deviation of $\bar{x}_\text{HS}-\bar{x}_\text{Uni}$?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
###Independence conditions

To use this formula for $\sigma_{\bar{x}_\text{HS}-\bar{x}_\text{Uni}}$, we need two types of independence:

1. **Independent samples**: This formula relies on adding the variances of $\bar{x}_\text{HS}$ and $\bar{x}_\text{Uni}$, and we learned that we can only add variances when we have independent random variables. In other words, knowledge of one sample shouldn't give us extra information about the other sample.
2. **Independent observations within each sample**: Each observation in a sample shouldn't give us extra information about the next. If we're sampling without replacement, and we're sampling less than $10\%$ of the population, we can treat members in our sample as independent from each other. We call this the $10\%$ condition.

In this setting, are the samples independent? Are the members in each sample independent?

###Independent samples

Administrators are taking a single sample, then looking at two GPAs for each student in the sample. A student with a high GPA in high school may be likely to also have a high GPA in their first year at the university.

So we **cannot** treat the samples as independent of each other.

###Answer

It is not appropriate to use this standard deviation formula because the samples are not independent of each other.

[[☃ explanation 1]]

choices.content: The samples are not independent of each other.
We cannot assume independence between the high school GPAs in the sample.
We cannot assume independence between the university GPAs in the sample.

assessment_items.question_content:
Suraj recruits $50$ of the approximately $1{,}000$ students from his school to participate in a study. One-by-one, he records each subject's resting pulse rate (subjects don't interact with each other). Then, Suraj remotely makes his phone ring in his backpack. After he silences his phone, he records each subject's pulse rate again.

He plans to look at the difference between the mean "before" heart rates and the mean "after" heart rates $(\bar{x}_\text{A}-\bar{x}_\text{B})$ for the subjects in his sample.

Consider the formula:  

$\sigma_{\bar{x}_1-\bar{x}_2}=\sqrt{\dfrac{\sigma_1^2}{n_1}+\dfrac{\sigma_\text{2}^2}{n_2}}$

**Why is it not appropriate to use this formula for the standard deviation of $\bar{x}_\text{A}-\bar{x}_\text{B}$?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
###Independence conditions

To use this formula for $\sigma_{\bar{x}_\text{A}-\bar{x}_\text{B}}$, we need two types of independence:

1. **Independent samples**: This formula relies on adding the variances of $\bar{x}_\text{A}$ and $\bar{x}_\text{B}$, and we learned that we can only add variances when we have independent random variables. In other words, knowledge of one sample shouldn't give us extra information about the other sample.
2. **Independent observations within each sample**: Each observation in a sample shouldn't give us extra information about the next. If we're sampling without replacement, and we're sampling less than $10\%$ of the population, we can treat members in our sample as independent from each other. We call this the $10\%$ condition.

In this setting, are the samples independent? Are the members in each sample independent?

###Independent samples

Suraj is taking a single sample, then looking at two heart rates for each student in the sample. A student with a high "before" heart rate may be likely to also have a high "after" rate.

So we **cannot** treat the samples as independent of each other.

###Answer

It is not appropriate to use this standard deviation formula because the samples are not independent of each other.

[[☃ explanation 1]]

choices.content: The samples are not independent of each other.
We cannot assume independence between the "before" heart rates in the sample.
We cannot assume independence between the "after" heart rates in the sample.

assessment_items.question_content:
Maria is a college professor, and she wonders how the scores compare on her midterm and her final. She teaches two sections of a course, and each section has $180$ students. She decides to take a random sample of $10$ students from each section. For those students, she'll calculate the mean midterm score and the mean final score. She plans to look at the difference $(\text{Final}-\text{Midterm})$ between those means.

Consider the formula:  

$\sigma_{\bar{x}_1-\bar{x}_2}=\sqrt{\dfrac{\sigma_1^2}{n_1}+\dfrac{\sigma_\text{2}^2}{n_2}}$

**Why is it not appropriate to use this formula for the standard deviation of $\bar{x}_\text{F}-\bar{x}_\text{M}$?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
###Independence conditions

To use this formula for $\sigma_{\bar{x}_\text{F}-\bar{x}_\text{M}}$, we need two types of independence:

1. **Independent samples**: This formula relies on adding the variances of $\bar{x}_\text{F}$ and $\bar{x}_\text{M}$, and we learned that we can only add variances when we have independent random variables. In other words, knowledge of one sample shouldn't give us extra information about the other sample.
2. **Independent observations within each sample**: Each observation in a sample shouldn't give us extra information about the next. If we're sampling without replacement, and we're sampling less than $10\%$ of the population, we can treat members in our sample as independent from each other. We call this the $10\%$ condition.

In this setting, are the samples independent? Are the members in each sample independent?

###Independent samples

Maria is taking a stratified sample, then looking at two scores for each student in the sample. A student with a high midterm score may be likely to also have a high final exam score.

So we **cannot** treat the samples as independent of each other.

###Answer

It is not appropriate to use this standard deviation formula because the samples are not independent of each other.

[[☃ explanation 1]]

choices.content: The samples are not independent of each other.
We cannot assume independence between the midterm scores in the sample.
We cannot assume independence between the final exam scores in the sample.
